name: Obstacle Course Builder
description: Learners design and navigate obstacle courses that require crawling under, jumping over, balancing along, weaving through, and climbing on various stations. Each session features a different course layout. Learners take turns as designers, navigators, and observers who give feedback. Builds spatial awareness, locomotor skills, problem-solving, and creative thinking while keeping every body moving.
learning_objectives: Practice multiple locomotor and non-locomotor skills in sequence
Demonstrate spatial awareness by navigating through designed spaces
Design courses that challenge different movement skills
Give constructive feedback to peers on course design
variations: Blindfold challenge: navigate with a partner giving verbal directions
Timed personal best: try to beat YOUR OWN previous time, never someone else's
Wheelchair-accessible: design courses that work for all mobility levels
Indoor version: use classroom furniture and hallways

name: Animal Movement Safari
description: Learners explore different locomotor patterns by moving like various animals: bear crawl (hands and feet), crab walk (backward on hands and feet), frog jump (deep squat jumps), kangaroo hop (two-foot jumps), cheetah sprint (fast running), elephant stomp (heavy steps), flamingo balance (one-leg stand), snake slither (belly crawl). Each animal teaches a different movement concept. Learners invent new animal movements and teach them to classmates.
learning_objectives: Perform a variety of locomotor patterns with different body positions
Identify which muscles and body parts are working in each movement
Create original movement patterns inspired by animal locomotion
Transition smoothly between different movement types
variations: Habitat zones: set up different areas as ocean, jungle, savanna — different animals in each
Movement freeze: call out an animal, everyone transitions to that movement
Science connection: discuss how animal body structures enable their movements
Pair work: one partner moves, the other guesses the animal

name: Heart Rate Explorer
description: Learners measure their resting heart rate, then perform four activities of increasing intensity — walking, brisk walking, jogging, and jumping jacks — measuring heart rate after each. They graph the results, calculate recovery time (how long to return to resting rate), and discuss what they discover about how exercise intensity affects the cardiovascular system. Introduces target heart rate zones and the concept of aerobic vs. anaerobic exercise.
materials: Graph paper or digital graphing tool
Optional: pulse oximeter or fitness tracker
learning_objectives: Measure heart rate accurately using pulse points
Describe how exercise intensity affects heart rate
Graph heart rate data and identify patterns
Explain the concept of recovery time and target heart rate zones
variations: Compare resting heart rates before and after a regular exercise program over weeks
Add breathing rate measurements alongside heart rate
Research: what is a healthy resting heart rate for your age?
Advanced: calculate target heart rate zones using the Karvonen formula

name: Muscle Map — Name That Muscle
description: Using a large body outline (drawn on butcher paper or projected), learners label major muscle groups: quadriceps, hamstrings, calves, biceps, triceps, abdominals, obliques, deltoids, pectorals, trapezius, gluteus, and latissimus dorsi. Then they perform exercises that target each muscle group, placing a marker on the body map for each muscle used. Builds the connection between anatomy and movement.
materials: Large body outline on paper or poster
learning_objectives: Identify and locate major muscle groups on the body
Connect specific exercises to the muscles they target
Understand that most movements involve multiple muscle groups
Design a simple workout that targets all major muscle groups
variations: Younger learners: focus on 6 major groups (arms, legs, core, back, chest, shoulders)
Advanced: add muscle actions (flexion, extension, rotation, abduction, adduction)
Team challenge: each team designs a full-body workout with at least one exercise per muscle group
Art integration: draw or sculpt muscles on the body outline

name: Modified Kickball for All
description: A reimagined version of kickball designed for maximum inclusion: everyone kicks every inning (no outs), scoring counts total bases reached by the whole team (not individual runs), bases are accessible for wheelchair users, a buddy system pairs younger or less experienced players with mentors, and rule modifications allow different ways to 'kick' (foot, hand push for wheelchair users, rolling for younger children). Focus is on participation, strategy, and fun — not elimination.
learning_objectives: Participate in a team sport with modified rules for inclusion
Practice kicking, throwing, catching, and base running skills
Demonstrate sportsmanship regardless of game outcome
Understand how rule modifications make games more inclusive
variations: Silent kickball: no talking — all communication through gestures and signals
Strategy kickball: each team gets a 2-minute strategy session before each inning
Reverse kickball: fielding team kicks, kicking team fields — experience both roles
Math kickball: bases are worth different points based on math problems solved

name: Team Strategy Challenge
description: Small teams (4-6 players) receive a game challenge — capture the flag, territory control, or a custom objective game. Before playing, teams have 5 minutes to plan strategy on paper: draw positions, assign roles, plan plays. Then they play for 10 minutes. After, they debrief: what worked? What failed? What would you change? Then they revise their strategy and play again. Teaches strategic thinking, communication, adaptation, and the difference between a plan and its execution.
materials: Whiteboard or paper for strategy planning
learning_objectives: Develop and communicate a team strategy before game play
Execute a plan while adapting to opponents' actions
Analyze game performance and identify improvements
Practice leadership, communication, and cooperation in competitive settings
variations: Constraint challenge: each round adds a rule (e.g., no running, must pass to 3 people before scoring)
Role rotation: every player must play every position across rounds
Video review: record gameplay and analyze tactics as a team
Cross-team debrief: teams share strategies with each other after final round

name: Rhythm & Freeze Dance
description: Music plays, everyone dances freely. When the music stops, everyone freezes in a creative pose. Each round adds movement constraints: move only in slow motion, move only at a low level (near the floor), move like you're underwater, move using only your arms, move like a robot, move smoothly like flowing water. Builds body control, listening skills, creative expression, and the ability to move in different qualities. No wrong way to dance — every interpretation is valid.
materials: Music player with diverse music genres
Optional: scarves or ribbons for expressive movement
learning_objectives: Move creatively in response to music
Demonstrate body control by freezing on cue
Explore different movement qualities (smooth, sharp, slow, fast, heavy, light)
Express ideas and emotions through movement without self-consciousness
variations: Genre rotation: play different music styles — classical, hip-hop, folk, electronic — and notice how movement changes
Partner freeze: when music stops, quickly find a partner and create a joint pose
Storytelling freeze: each freeze tells part of a story the group invents together
Emotion dance: call out emotions and dancers express them through movement

name: Cultural Dance Sampler
description: Learners experience basic steps from four to six different cultural dance traditions over multiple sessions. Examples: West African dance (polyrhythmic, grounded, expressive), Irish step dance (precise footwork, upright posture), Mexican folk dance (partner formations, skirt work), Indian classical dance (hand gestures, facial expression, footwork), Brazilian capoeira (martial arts dance fusion), Japanese Bon Odori (community circle dance). Each dance is introduced with cultural context: where it comes from, what it means, when it is performed, and why it matters to its community.
learning_objectives: Perform basic steps from at least four cultural dance traditions
Describe the cultural context and significance of each dance form
Demonstrate respect for dance traditions different from one's own
Identify commonalities and differences across dance traditions
variations: Invite community members from different cultural backgrounds to teach their dance traditions
Research project: learners research a dance tradition and present to the class
Fusion challenge: create a short piece combining elements from two traditions (with cultural sensitivity discussion)
Performance showcase: each group performs a cultural dance for families and community

name: Personal Fitness Plan Builder
description: The capstone application activity: learners design a complete weekly fitness plan using the FITT principle. They choose activities they genuinely enjoy, set realistic goals (SMART format), schedule workouts around their actual life, plan for barriers (weather, schedule conflicts, low motivation), and include warm-up, cool-down, and rest days. They follow the plan for two weeks, log their adherence and how they feel, then reflect and revise. The plan is assessed on quality and realism, not on athletic performance.
materials: Activity menu (list of options across all five fitness components)
learning_objectives: Design a personal fitness plan using the FITT principle
Set SMART fitness goals (Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound)
Follow a fitness plan for two weeks and track adherence
Reflect on what worked, what didn't, and revise the plan based on experience
variations: Buddy system: pairs create plans and check in with each other for accountability
Family plan: design a fitness plan that includes family members
No-equipment plan: all activities must require zero equipment (bodyweight, walking, running)
Seasonal adaptation: create different plans for summer vs. winter vs. rainy days

name: Healthy Habits Tracker
description: Learners track three wellness habits for one week: daily movement (type and duration), sleep (hours and quality), and one additional metric of their choice (water intake, screen time, mood rating, stress level, fruit/vegetable servings). At the end of the week, they look for patterns: does more movement correlate with better sleep? Does screen time affect mood? Does hydration affect energy? They create a simple data visualization and write a reflection connecting their findings to the science behind movement and wellness.
materials: Graph paper or spreadsheet for visualization
learning_objectives: Track wellness habits accurately over one week
Identify patterns and correlations in personal health data
Create a data visualization showing wellness patterns
Connect personal observations to exercise science and wellness research
variations: Extended tracking: continue for a full month to see longer-term patterns
Class data: aggregate anonymous class data to find group patterns (with permission)
Intervention experiment: change one habit for a week and track the effect
Connection to NUTR-101: add nutrition tracking and explore food-movement connections
